full_response:
For purely radial motion the Schwarzschild line-element can be written with the tortoise coordinate  

 r_* = r + 2GM ln|r/2GM – 1|  

as  
 ds² = (1 – 2GM/r)( – dt² + dr_*² ).             (1)

For a light ray ds² = 0, so

 dt = ± dr_* .                                        (2)

Define the null (light-cone) coordinates  

 u ≡ t – r_*  (retarded time)  
 v ≡ t + r_*  (advanced time).                     (3)

Take differentials:

 du = dt – dr_* ,     dv = dt + dr_* .              (4)

Outgoing rays (moving toward larger r).  
Choose the ‘+’ sign in (2): dt = + dr_*  (dr_* increases, hence r increases).  
Insert in (4):

 du = dr_* – dr_* = 0  ⇒ u = const.,  
 dv = dr_* + dr_* = 2 dr_* ≠ 0.

Thus u is constant along outward-moving (larger-r) light rays.

Ingoing rays (moving toward smaller r).  
Choose the ‘–’ sign in (2): dt = – dr_*  (dr_* decreases, hence r decreases).  
Insert in (4):

 dv = –dr_* + dr_* = 0  ⇒ v = const.,  
 du = –dr_* – dr_* = –2 dr_* ≠ 0.

Thus v is constant along inward-moving (smaller-r) light rays.

\boxed{\;u = t - r_* \text{ is constant for light rays moving toward larger } r\;,\quad
       v = t + r_* \text{ is constant for light rays moving toward smaller } r\;}

Confidence: 90%
extracted_answer:
\;u = t - r_* \text{ is constant for light rays moving toward larger } r\;,\quad
       v = t + r_* \text{ is constant for light rays moving toward smaller } r\;
